what_is_it: A group of ingredients that help oil and water stay mixed instead of separating.
why_used: They make food smoother, creamier, and more stable.
fun_fact: Lecithins are often used in chocolate, spreads, and creamy sauces.
health_impact_human: Usually fine in small amounts, but they still signal a more processed product.
health_impact_dog: Usually okay for dogs in small amounts and commonly used to keep texture smooth.
health_impact_cat: Usually okay for cats in small amounts and mainly used for texture, not nutrition.

what_is_it: A plant-based emulsifier made from oats that helps different ingredients blend together.
why_used: It keeps products smooth and helps fat and water mix more evenly.
fun_fact: It is often seen as a plant-based alternative to soy lecithin.
health_impact_human: Generally low concern for people, but it is still part of processed food.
health_impact_dog: Usually okay for dogs in small amounts and mostly used to improve texture.
health_impact_cat: Usually okay for cats in small amounts and mainly helps keep food evenly mixed.

what_is_it: A sour ingredient found naturally in citrus fruits and also made for food use.
why_used: It adds a tangy taste and helps keep food fresh for longer.
fun_fact: It is one of the most common additives in drinks, sweets, and sauces.
health_impact_human: Generally considered safe for most people and widely used in everyday foods.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs in small food amounts, though very sour products may upset sensitive stomachs.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats in small food amounts, but it is not something they need in large amounts.

what_is_it: A thickener from red seaweed that helps food stay smooth and creamy.
why_used: It improves texture and stops ingredients from separating.
fun_fact: Seaweed-based thickeners have been used in cooking for a very long time.
health_impact_human: Some people are okay with it, but others prefer to limit it because it is debated in digestion-related discussions.
health_impact_dog: Often used in pet food and usually tolerated, but some dogs with sensitive stomachs may do better with less of it.
health_impact_cat: Often used in pet food, but some cats with sensitive digestion may not tolerate it well.

what_is_it: A thickener made from guar beans that helps food feel smooth and evenly mixed.
why_used: It improves texture and helps sauces, desserts, and spreads stay consistent.
fun_fact: Even a small amount can make a product feel much thicker.
health_impact_human: Usually tolerated well, but many people prefer fewer gums in highly processed foods.
health_impact_dog: Usually okay for dogs in small amounts and commonly used to help texture.
health_impact_cat: Usually okay for cats in small amounts, though too much may be harder on sensitive digestion.

what_is_it: A sweet ingredient made from cocoa beans, often mixed with sugar and milk.
why_used: It is used for its rich flavor, creamy texture, and comforting taste.
fun_fact: Chocolate was once so valuable that cocoa beans were used like money in ancient cultures.
health_impact_human: Usually fine for people in moderate amounts, especially darker chocolate, but too much can mean lots of sugar and calories.
health_impact_dog: Dangerous for dogs because it contains theobromine, which their bodies process very slowly.
health_impact_cat: Also unsafe for cats because compounds in chocolate can affect the nervous system and heart.

what_is_it: A sharp-tasting vegetable that adds depth and strong flavor to food.
why_used: It is used to make meals smell richer and taste more savory.
fun_fact: Cutting onions makes your eyes water because they release a gas that turns into a mild acid in your eyes.
health_impact_human: Fine for most people and even linked to helpful plant compounds when eaten in normal amounts.
health_impact_dog: Unsafe for dogs because it can damage red blood cells and cause serious illness.
health_impact_cat: Toxic for cats, and even small amounts can be harmful.

what_is_it: A sugar substitute that tastes sweet but has fewer calories than regular sugar.
why_used: It is used to sweeten products like chewing gum, candy, and some low-sugar foods.
fun_fact: Xylitol may sound like a lab chemical, but it can also be found naturally in tiny amounts in some fruits and vegetables.
health_impact_human: Generally safe for humans in normal food amounts, though too much may upset the stomach.
health_impact_dog: Very dangerous for dogs because it can cause a rapid insulin spike, low blood sugar, and even liver failure.
health_impact_cat: Not known to be as dangerous for cats as for dogs, but it should still be avoided because it offers no benefit and may cause stomach upset.

what_is_it: A red food coloring made from crushed cochineal insects.
why_used: It is used to give foods and drinks a bright pink or red color.
fun_fact: Yes, this color really can come from insects — which makes it one of the most surprising ingredients on many labels.
health_impact_human: Approved for human use, but some people avoid it for ethical, dietary, or allergy reasons.
health_impact_dog: Usually not considered harmful to dogs in the small amounts used as coloring, but it adds color, not value.
health_impact_cat: Usually not considered harmful to cats in very small food-coloring amounts, but it has no nutritional benefit.

what_is_it: A group of ingredients that help oil and water stay mixed instead of separating.
why_used: They are used to keep food smooth, creamy, and stable.
fun_fact: Lecithins are the quiet teamwork ingredient behind many chocolates, spreads, and creamy products.
health_impact_human: Usually fine in small amounts, but they still signal a more processed product.
health_impact_dog: Usually okay for dogs in small food amounts and mainly used for texture.
health_impact_cat: Usually okay for cats in small food amounts and mostly included to keep texture consistent.

what_is_it: An oil extracted from the seeds of the oil palm fruit.
why_used: It is used to improve texture and make products creamy, stable, and longer-lasting.
fun_fact: Palm oil is one of the most widely used ingredients in the world — it’s in everything from cookies to cosmetics.
health_impact_human: Common in processed foods and safe in small amounts, but often linked to highly processed products and environmental concerns.
health_impact_dog: Not toxic for dogs, but high fat content can be hard to digest and may contribute to weight gain.
health_impact_cat: Not toxic for cats, but offers little nutritional value and is mainly used for texture.

what_is_it: Proteins naturally found in milk, such as casein and whey.
why_used: They are used to improve nutritional value and texture in dairy and processed foods.
fun_fact: Milk proteins are often added to boost protein content in snacks and drinks.
health_impact_human: Good source of protein for most people unless there is lactose intolerance or allergy.
health_impact_dog: Generally safe for dogs in small amounts, especially in pet food formulas.
health_impact_cat: Cats can digest some milk proteins, but many are lactose intolerant, so dairy-based ingredients may cause issues.

what_is_it: A synthetic blue food coloring also known as E133.
why_used: It is used to give food and drinks a bright, eye-catching blue color.
fun_fact: Bright blue food doesn’t exist much in nature — that’s why this color is almost always artificial.
health_impact_human: Approved for use, but some people prefer to avoid artificial colorings in highly processed foods.
health_impact_dog: Generally considered safe for dogs in small amounts, but has no nutritional value.
health_impact_cat: Generally considered safe for cats in very small amounts, but used only for appearance.

what_is_it: A mix of natural or artificial substances that give food its smell and taste.
why_used: They are used to enhance or imitate flavors, making products more appealing.
fun_fact: “Natural flavor” doesn’t always mean simple — it can still be a complex mix of many compounds.
health_impact_human: Usually safe, but often unclear what exactly is included, which can reduce transparency.
health_impact_dog: Generally safe for dogs in small amounts, but composition is often unspecified.
health_impact_cat: Generally safe for cats in small amounts, but lacks transparency and nutritional value.

what_is_it: An emulsifier made from fatty acids that helps ingredients mix together smoothly.
why_used: It is used to improve texture and keep food stable and evenly mixed.
fun_fact: Emulsifiers like this are the reason your food doesn’t separate into layers.
health_impact_human: Considered safe, but typically found in processed foods.
health_impact_dog: Generally safe for dogs in small amounts and mainly used for texture.
health_impact_cat: Generally safe for cats in small amounts and has no direct health effect.

what_is_it: A simple carbohydrate used to sweeten food and drinks.
why_used: It is added to improve taste and increase product appeal.
fun_fact: Sugar can appear under many different names, making it harder to recognize on labels.
health_impact_human: Fine in small amounts, but high intake is linked to many health issues.
health_impact_dog: Not toxic for dogs, but unnecessary and can contribute to obesity and dental problems.
health_impact_cat: Not needed in a cat’s diet and offers no nutritional benefit.

what_is_it: A dairy by-product obtained during cheese production.
why_used: It is used to add flavor, protein, and improve texture in foods.
fun_fact: Whey was once considered waste — now it’s widely used in food and supplements.
health_impact_human: Provides protein, but may cause issues for people with lactose intolerance.
health_impact_dog: Generally safe for dogs in small amounts and often used in pet food.
health_impact_cat: Cats may have trouble digesting dairy components, so tolerance can vary.

what_is_it: A starch that has been physically or chemically altered to change its behavior.
why_used: It is used to thicken, stabilize, and improve the texture of food.
fun_fact: Despite the name, modified starch is not necessarily harmful — it just behaves differently than regular starch.
health_impact_human: Generally safe, but indicates a more processed product.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs in small amounts and commonly used in pet food.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats in small amounts and mainly affects texture.

what_is_it: Milk that has had most of its fat removed and then dried into powder form.
why_used: It is used to add milk flavor and nutrients without extra fat.
fun_fact: Powdered milk lasts much longer than fresh milk, which makes it useful in many products.
health_impact_human: Good source of protein and nutrients, but may cause issues for lactose-intolerant individuals.
health_impact_dog: Generally safe for dogs in small amounts.
health_impact_cat: Many cats are lactose intolerant, so this ingredient may cause digestive discomfort.

what_is_it: A yellow spice made from the root of the turmeric plant.
why_used: It is used for color, flavor, and sometimes for its perceived health properties.
fun_fact: Turmeric has been used for centuries in cooking and traditional medicine.
health_impact_human: Generally safe and often associated with anti-inflammatory properties.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs in small amounts and sometimes used in pet diets.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats in small amounts, but not essential.

what_is_it: A thickening agent made from guar beans.
why_used: It is used to improve texture and keep ingredients evenly mixed.
fun_fact: Guar gum can absorb many times its weight in water, which helps create creamy textures.
health_impact_human: Usually tolerated well, but some people prefer to limit gums in processed foods.
health_impact_dog: Generally safe for dogs, but large amounts may affect digestion.
health_impact_cat: Generally safe for cats in small amounts, but excessive intake may cause digestive issues.

what_is_it: A thickener created through natural fermentation using bacteria.
why_used: It helps food become thicker and keeps ingredients evenly mixed.
fun_fact: Xanthan gum is widely used in gluten-free products because it helps replace the texture of gluten.
health_impact_human: Generally considered safe, but often found in processed foods and sometimes avoided by people sensitive to gums.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs in small amounts, but large amounts may cause digestive discomfort.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats in small amounts, but too much may be harder to digest.

what_is_it: A natural thickener found in fruits like apples and citrus peels.
why_used: It helps jams, desserts, and fillings set and keep their shape.
fun_fact: Pectin is what makes jam thick without needing artificial chemicals.
health_impact_human: Naturally occurring and generally safe, often seen as a more “natural” thickening option.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs and sometimes even used to support digestion in small amounts.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats in small amounts and generally easy to tolerate.

what_is_it: Processed fats that help water and oil stay mixed in food.
why_used: They improve texture and keep products soft, smooth, and stable.
fun_fact: These ingredients are often made from vegetable oils but go through multiple processing steps.
health_impact_human: Generally safe, but highly processed and commonly found in industrial food products.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs in small amounts and mainly used to improve texture.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats in small amounts and has no direct nutritional benefit.

what_is_it: An emulsifier made from fats combined with citric acid.
why_used: It helps keep food smooth and prevents ingredients from separating.
fun_fact: Despite the complex name, its job is simple: keeping food from falling apart.
health_impact_human: Considered safe, but typically found in processed foods and not needed in simple diets.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs in small amounts and mainly affects texture.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats in small amounts and used only for stability.

what_is_it: A highly processed emulsifier used mainly in chocolate.
why_used: It makes chocolate smoother, thinner, and easier to work with during production.
fun_fact: Without ingredients like PGPR, your chocolate bar might feel thicker and less smooth — it’s part of what gives chocolate that melt-in-your-mouth texture.
health_impact_human: Generally considered safe in small amounts, but it is a highly processed additive.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs in very small amounts, but mainly adds texture, not nutrition.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats in small amounts and has no nutritional value.

what_is_it: A group of mineral salts commonly used in baking, including baking soda.
why_used: They help dough rise and create light, fluffy textures in baked goods.
fun_fact: These ingredients are behind the bubbles that make cakes soft and airy.
health_impact_human: Generally safe and widely used in everyday cooking and baking.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs in small food amounts.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats in small food amounts and not harmful in typical use.

what_is_it: A flavour enhancer that boosts savory taste, also known as MSG.
why_used: It makes food taste richer, deeper, and more “umami”.
fun_fact: MSG has been controversial for years, but most scientific reviews consider it safe in normal amounts.
health_impact_human: Generally safe, but some people report sensitivity or prefer to avoid it.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs in small amounts, but adds flavor rather than nutrition.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats in small amounts and mainly enhances taste.

what_is_it: A very strong artificial sweetener that adds sweetness without sugar.
why_used: It is used in diet drinks and low-sugar foods to replace sugar.
fun_fact: Acesulfame K is much sweeter than sugar, so only tiny amounts are needed.
health_impact_human: Approved for use, but often avoided by people who prefer fewer artificial sweeteners.
health_impact_dog: Not toxic for dogs, but offers no benefit and is not recommended in regular diets.
health_impact_cat: Not useful for cats and adds no nutritional value.

what_is_it: A low-calorie artificial sweetener used in many diet products.
why_used: It replaces sugar to reduce calories while keeping sweetness.
fun_fact: Aspartame is one of the most debated food ingredients, even though it is approved by regulators.
health_impact_human: Considered safe in regulated amounts, but still widely debated.
health_impact_dog: Not beneficial for dogs and should not be a regular part of their diet.
health_impact_cat: Not useful for cats and adds no nutritional value.

what_is_it: A group of artificial sweeteners used to add sweetness without calories.
why_used: They are often used in tabletop sweeteners and diet products.
fun_fact: Cyclamates were once banned in some countries, which still makes them a topic of debate today.
health_impact_human: Approved in many regions, but often avoided by people who prefer more natural options.
health_impact_dog: Not toxic for dogs in small amounts, but unnecessary and not recommended.
health_impact_cat: Not useful for cats and offers no nutritional value.

what_is_it: A sugar alcohol used as a low-calorie sweetener.
why_used: It provides sweetness with fewer calories than regular sugar.
fun_fact: Isomalt is often used in sugar-free candies and can create that slightly “cooling” taste.
health_impact_human: Generally safe, but large amounts can cause digestive discomfort.
health_impact_dog: Not toxic for dogs, but can cause stomach upset if eaten in larger amounts.
health_impact_cat: Not needed for cats and may affect digestion if consumed in larger amounts.

what_is_it: A very strong artificial sweetener used to replace sugar.
why_used: It adds sweetness without adding calories.
fun_fact: Saccharin is one of the oldest artificial sweeteners still in use today.
health_impact_human: Approved for use, but often avoided by people who prefer fewer artificial ingredients.
health_impact_dog: Not toxic for dogs, but provides no benefit and is best limited.
health_impact_cat: Not useful for cats and has no nutritional role.

what_is_it: A high-intensity sweetener made from sugar through chemical processing.
why_used: It keeps food sweet without adding much energy.
fun_fact: Sucralose stays sweet even when heated, which makes it useful in baking.
health_impact_human: Considered safe, but often avoided by people reducing artificial sweeteners.
health_impact_dog: Not toxic for dogs in small amounts, but unnecessary and not recommended.
health_impact_cat: Not useful for cats and offers no nutritional benefit.

what_is_it: A sweetener extracted from the stevia plant.
why_used: It provides sweetness without regular sugar.
fun_fact: Stevia-based sweeteners can be many times sweeter than sugar, even in tiny amounts.
health_impact_human: Often seen as a more natural option, but taste and tolerance vary.
health_impact_dog: Generally safe for dogs in small amounts.
health_impact_cat: Generally safe for cats in small amounts, though not nutritionally needed.

what_is_it: A group of sugar alcohol sweeteners used to replace regular sugar.
why_used: They add sweetness with fewer calories and are often used in sugar-free products.
fun_fact: Maltitols are common in “sugar-free” chocolate — but eating too much can still have noticeable effects.
health_impact_human: Generally safe, but large amounts can cause bloating or digestive discomfort.
health_impact_dog: Not toxic for dogs, but may cause stomach upset and offers no real benefit.
health_impact_cat: Not needed in a cat’s diet and may affect digestion if consumed in larger amounts.

what_is_it: A low-calorie sugar alcohol used as a sweetener.
why_used: It provides sweetness without regular sugar and is popular in low-carb products.
fun_fact: Erythritol is often better tolerated than other sugar alcohols, but too much can still upset digestion.
health_impact_human: Generally safe, but large amounts may cause digestive discomfort.
health_impact_dog: Not toxic for dogs in small amounts, but unnecessary and may upset digestion.
health_impact_cat: Not useful for cats and offers no nutritional benefit.

what_is_it: A form of vitamin C used in food.
why_used: It helps keep products fresh and can support stability in food processing.
fun_fact: Despite its technical name, this is simply vitamin C — something your body actually needs.
health_impact_human: Generally safe and even beneficial as a vitamin in normal amounts.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs and sometimes included in pet food for stability.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats in small amounts and not harmful in typical use.

what_is_it: A sodium-based form of vitamin C.
why_used: It helps keep food fresh and stable, especially in processed products.
fun_fact: It’s basically vitamin C with a slight chemical twist to make it easier to use in food.
health_impact_human: Generally safe and functions similarly to vitamin C.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs in small amounts and commonly used in food processing.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats in small amounts and not harmful.

what_is_it: A natural antioxidant related to vitamin E.
why_used: It protects fats and oils from going bad.
fun_fact: Tocopherols are often used as a more “natural” alternative to synthetic preservatives.
health_impact_human: Generally safe and often seen as a positive additive compared to synthetic options.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs and sometimes used to preserve pet food naturally.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats and commonly used to protect fats in food.

what_is_it: A synthetic antioxidant used to keep fats from spoiling.
why_used: It helps extend shelf life in processed foods.
fun_fact: BHA has been debated for years, which is why many people prefer to limit it.
health_impact_human: Approved for use, but often limited by people trying to avoid synthetic additives.
health_impact_dog: Allowed in pet food, but many prefer to limit synthetic preservatives.
health_impact_cat: Used in some pet foods, but often avoided in more premium diets.

what_is_it: A synthetic antioxidant similar to BHA.
why_used: It prevents fats from going rancid and extends shelf life.
fun_fact: BHT is often mentioned together with BHA in discussions about food additives.
health_impact_human: Approved, but often limited by people avoiding synthetic preservatives.
health_impact_dog: Used in pet food, but many owners prefer more natural alternatives.
health_impact_cat: Common in some pet foods, but often avoided in higher-quality diets.

what_is_it: A preservative that stops mold and yeast from growing.
why_used: It helps food stay safe and last longer.
fun_fact: Without preservatives like this, many foods would spoil much faster.
health_impact_human: Generally safe, but some people prefer fewer preservatives in their diet.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs in small amounts and commonly used in food preservation.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats in small amounts and helps prevent spoilage.

what_is_it: A widely used preservative that prevents mold and yeast.
why_used: It extends the shelf life of many packaged foods.
fun_fact: It is one of the most common preservatives you’ll find on food labels.
health_impact_human: Approved for use, but often limited by people seeking less processed food.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs in small amounts and commonly used in food products.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats in small amounts and helps keep food fresh.

what_is_it: A preservative used to slow down the growth of bacteria, yeast, and mold.
why_used: It helps food and drinks stay fresh for longer.
fun_fact: It works best in acidic foods like soft drinks and fruit products.
health_impact_human: Generally safe, but many people prefer fewer preservatives in everyday food.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs in small amounts and mainly used to prevent spoilage.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats in small amounts and helps keep food stable.

what_is_it: A preservative made from benzoic acid, commonly used in drinks.
why_used: It helps prevent spoilage and keeps products safe to consume longer.
fun_fact: It is especially common in soft drinks, sauces, and packaged foods.
health_impact_human: Approved for use, but often limited by people trying to reduce processed additives.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs in small amounts and used to preserve food.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats in small amounts and helps prevent spoilage.

what_is_it: A preservative gas used to protect food from spoilage and discoloration.
why_used: It helps keep foods like dried fruit and wine looking fresh and stable.
fun_fact: Some people are sensitive to sulphites, which is why this ingredient is often highlighted on labels.
health_impact_human: Generally safe, but can cause reactions in sensitive individuals.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs in small amounts, but not needed in fresh diets.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats in small amounts, though unnecessary in most diets.

what_is_it: A curing agent used in processed meats.
why_used: It preserves meat and keeps its pink color.
fun_fact: Without it, many processed meats would look grey instead of pink.
health_impact_human: Often recommended to limit because it is linked to processed meat consumption.
health_impact_dog: Used in some pet foods, but generally better limited in frequent diets.
health_impact_cat: Not ideal for regular consumption in cats and often limited in higher-quality diets.

what_is_it: A preservative used in curing and preserving meats.
why_used: It helps extend shelf life and supports the curing process.
fun_fact: It can convert into other compounds during processing or digestion.
health_impact_human: Often limited due to its link with processed meat products.
health_impact_dog: Not toxic for dogs in small amounts, but not ideal for regular consumption.
health_impact_cat: Not recommended in frequent cat diets and mainly used in processed foods.

what_is_it: The main acid found in vinegar.
why_used: It adds a sour taste and helps preserve food.
fun_fact: This “chemical” name is actually just vinegar in disguise.
health_impact_human: Generally safe and commonly used in everyday foods.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs in small amounts and often found in food.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats in small amounts and not harmful in typical use.

what_is_it: An acid naturally produced during fermentation.
why_used: It helps control acidity and preserve food.
fun_fact: It is the same process that gives yogurt and fermented foods their characteristic taste.
health_impact_human: Generally safe and naturally present in many foods.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs and sometimes used in food processing.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats and not harmful in typical amounts.

what_is_it: A gas used to create bubbles in drinks or protect food in packaging.
why_used: It adds fizz to drinks and helps extend shelf life.
fun_fact: It’s the same gas that makes sparkling water bubbly.
health_impact_human: Generally safe and widely used in drinks and packaging.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs in small amounts, though fizzy drinks are not recommended.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats in small amounts, but fizzy products are not suitable for them.

what_is_it: An acid commonly used in soft drinks, especially cola.
why_used: It adds a sharp, tangy taste and helps control acidity.
fun_fact: It is one of the reasons cola has its distinctive flavor.
health_impact_human: Generally safe, but often limited in highly processed drinks.
health_impact_dog: Not toxic for dogs in small amounts, but not recommended in regular diets.
health_impact_cat: Not useful for cats and offers no nutritional benefit.

what_is_it: A plant-based extract used as a natural antioxidant.
why_used: It helps protect fats and oils from going bad.
fun_fact: Even though it comes from rosemary, it is used more for preservation than flavor.
health_impact_human: Generally safe and often seen as a more natural alternative to synthetic preservatives.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs and sometimes used in higher-quality pet food.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats and commonly used to protect food freshness.

what_is_it: A group of sugar alcohol sweeteners used instead of regular sugar.
why_used: They add sweetness and help keep products moist.
fun_fact: Sorbitol is often used in “sugar-free” products — but that doesn’t mean you can eat unlimited amounts.
health_impact_human: Generally safe, but large amounts can cause bloating or digestive discomfort.
health_impact_dog: Not toxic for dogs, but can cause stomach upset and has no real benefit.
health_impact_cat: Not needed in a cat’s diet and may affect digestion if consumed in larger amounts.

what_is_it: A sugar alcohol used as a sweetener and to add structure to food.
why_used: It helps create sweetness and improve texture in sugar-free products.
fun_fact: Mannitol is sometimes used as a coating in sweets because it creates a slightly cooling sensation.
health_impact_human: Generally safe, but large amounts may lead to digestive discomfort.
health_impact_dog: Not toxic for dogs, but may upset digestion and is not nutritionally useful.
health_impact_cat: Not useful for cats and may cause digestive discomfort in larger amounts.

what_is_it: A syrup-like ingredient that helps food stay soft and moist.
why_used: It prevents products from drying out and improves texture.
fun_fact: Glycerol is also used in skincare — it’s great at holding onto moisture.
health_impact_human: Generally safe and commonly used in many foods.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs in small amounts and mainly used to maintain texture.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats in small amounts and helps keep food moist.

what_is_it: A processed emulsifier that helps oil and water stay mixed.
why_used: It keeps food smooth, stable, and evenly blended.
fun_fact: Polysorbate 80 is widely used in processed foods, sauces, and desserts.
health_impact_human: Approved for use, but often avoided by people limiting highly processed additives.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs in small amounts, but mainly adds texture, not nutrition.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats in small amounts, but unnecessary in a simple diet.

what_is_it: An emulsifier used to keep food smooth and evenly mixed.
why_used: It improves texture and prevents ingredients from separating.
fun_fact: It belongs to a group of additives designed to make processed foods more stable.
health_impact_human: Considered safe, but typically found in more processed products.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs in small amounts, but has no nutritional value.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats in small amounts and mainly affects texture.

what_is_it: An emulsifier that helps create smooth and creamy textures.
why_used: It keeps ingredients blended together in processed foods.
fun_fact: It is commonly used in products like ice cream to improve consistency.
health_impact_human: Generally safe, but often limited in diets focused on less processed foods.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs in small amounts and mainly used for texture.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats in small amounts and not nutritionally relevant.

what_is_it: A less common emulsifier used to stabilize processed foods.
why_used: It helps maintain texture and prevent separation of ingredients.
fun_fact: It is part of the same family as other polysorbates, which are widely used in food production.
health_impact_human: Approved for use, but associated with more processed foods.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs in small amounts, but not nutritionally needed.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats in small amounts and mainly affects texture.

what_is_it: A natural thickener made from seaweed.
why_used: It is used to create gels, jellies, and plant-based desserts.
fun_fact: Agar is a popular vegan alternative to gelatin and has been used in Asian cuisine for centuries.
health_impact_human: Generally safe and often seen as a more natural thickening option.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs in small amounts and sometimes used in food.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats in small amounts and easy to tolerate.

what_is_it: A thickener made from the seeds of the carob tree.
why_used: It helps food stay creamy and improves texture.
fun_fact: It is commonly used in ice cream to prevent ice crystals and keep it smooth.
health_impact_human: Generally safe, but often associated with processed foods.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs in small amounts and helps improve texture.

health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats.

what_is_it: An organ involved in blood filtration.
why_used: Used as a source of iron and nutrients.
fun_fact: Less commonly used than other organs.
health_impact_human: Very rich in iron; beneficial in moderation.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats.

what_is_it: A flexible tissue found in joints.
why_used: Used to support joint health.
fun_fact: Often included in natural diets.
health_impact_human: Contains collagen that supports joints and connective tissue.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs and beneficial for joints.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats.

what_is_it: Soft tissue found inside bones.
why_used: Used as an energy-rich ingredient.
fun_fact: Very rich in fat.
health_impact_human: Highly calorie-dense; too much may lead to excess fat intake.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs in moderation.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats in moderation.

what_is_it: A bone filled with nutrient-rich marrow.
why_used: Used as a chew and source of fats and minerals.
fun_fact: Popular as a natural treat.
health_impact_human: Provides fats and minerals, but high in calories and should be given in moderation.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs as a chew when supervised.
health_impact_cat: Not ideal for cats due to hardness.

what_is_it: Fish broken down into very small protein pieces.
why_used: Used to improve digestibility and reduce allergies.
fun_fact: Common in sensitive diets.
health_impact_human: Highly processed but easier to digest and less likely to trigger allergies.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs and useful in allergy diets.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats.

what_is_it: A processed fish-based flavor enhancer.
why_used: Used to improve taste and smell of food.
fun_fact: Often sprayed onto kibble.
health_impact_human: Highly processed and mainly used for flavor, not nutrition.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats.

what_is_it: Small marine crustaceans rich in nutrients.
why_used: Used as a source of protein and omega-3 fats.
fun_fact: Often included in premium foods.
health_impact_human: Rich in omega-3 and antioxidants; supports heart and brain health.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs and beneficial.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats.

what_is_it: A concentrated powder made from krill.
why_used: Used to provide protein and omega fats.
fun_fact: Common in premium formulas.
health_impact_human: Nutritious but processed; concentrated source of fats and protein.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats.

what_is_it: A shellfish used as a protein source.
why_used: Used to provide minerals and nutrients.
fun_fact: Often found in premium recipes.
health_impact_human: Provides protein, minerals, and omega fats.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats.

what_is_it: A special type of mussel rich in joint-support compounds.
why_used: Used to support joint health.
fun_fact: Often added to supplements.
health_impact_human: Contains compounds that support joints and reduce inflammation.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs and beneficial for joints.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats.

what_is_it: A group of marine animals like shrimp or mussels.
why_used: Used as a protein and mineral source.
fun_fact: Common in seafood-based recipes.
health_impact_human: Rich in protein and minerals, but may trigger allergies in some people.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats.

what_is_it: A concentrated protein made from shrimp.
why_used: Used to add flavor and protein.
fun_fact: Often used in specialty foods.
health_impact_human: Nutritious but processed and can be high in minerals.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats.

what_is_it: A processed ingredient made from crab.
why_used: Used as a protein and mineral source.
fun_fact: Often used in premium or niche recipes.
health_impact_human: Nutritious but processed; may be high in minerals.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats.

what_is_it: A protein made from insects and used as an alternative to meat.
why_used: Used to boost protein while using fewer natural resources than traditional animal farming.
fun_fact: Many people do not realize insect protein is already showing up in modern pet food and some new human foods.
health_impact_human: It is rich in protein and often well tolerated, but people allergic to shellfish may react to it too because some of the proteins are similar. If it causes a problem, the reaction would usually be allergy-related, not toxicity-related.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs and often used in sensitive-diet formulas. If a dog reacts badly, it is more likely to be from food sensitivity than from the ingredient itself.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats and can work as an alternative protein source. Sensitive cats may still get mild digestive upset when trying a new protein.

what_is_it: A concentrated powder made from dried and processed insects.
why_used: Used to add a lot of protein in a small amount and help make dry food more nutrient-dense.
fun_fact: Because the water is removed, insect meal is much more concentrated than fresh insect ingredients.
health_impact_human: It can be nutritious, but it is more processed than a whole-food protein. The main concern is possible allergy reactions in sensitive people, especially those with shellfish allergy, and the fact that it is a very concentrated ingredient.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs and gives concentrated protein. In some dogs, a sudden change to a dense protein source may lead to gas, loose stool, or mild stomach upset.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats and can provide a compact protein source. Some cats may need time to adjust because concentrated ingredients can be harder on a sensitive stomach.

what_is_it: These are insect larvae used as a protein and fat source.
why_used: Used to add protein, fat, and minerals in a more sustainable way.
fun_fact: Black soldier fly larvae are one of the best-known insect ingredients in modern sustainable pet food.
health_impact_human: They are generally nutritious and well tolerated, with protein, fat, and minerals. The main possible issue is allergy sensitivity in people who already react to shellfish-like proteins, not a major safety problem for most people.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs and often a good option for dogs with food sensitivities. In some dogs, too much fat or a sudden diet change could still cause mild digestive upset.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats and can be a useful novel protein. Sensitive cats may still react to any new ingredient with vomiting or loose stool at first.

what_is_it: A small insect used as a source of protein and fat.
why_used: Used to add nutrients and provide an alternative to more common meats.
fun_fact: Mealworms are often used in novel-protein recipes because they are different from chicken or beef.
health_impact_human: Mealworm can provide good protein and healthy fats, but people with shellfish allergy may want extra caution because cross-reactions are possible. The main risk is allergy, not general toxicity.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs and often easy to digest. If a dog does not tolerate it, the most likely signs would be itching, soft stool, or mild stomach upset.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats and can be a useful protein option. As with any new protein, some cats may show digestive sensitivity at first.

what_is_it: An insect ingredient used as a protein source.
why_used: Used to add protein and nutrients, especially in specialty formulas.
fun_fact: Silkworm is still unusual in many markets, which is why it is often seen in more niche products.
health_impact_human: It can be a nutritious protein source and is not considered especially risky for most people. The main concern would be sensitivity or allergy in people who react to insect-related proteins.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs in balanced amounts. If a dog reacts, it would most likely be as a food sensitivity rather than a dangerous toxic effect.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats and generally well tolerated. Some cats may still get mild stomach upset when trying a new animal protein.

what_is_it: A liquid left behind when cheese is made.
why_used: Used to add protein and dairy nutrients, and sometimes to improve taste.
fun_fact: Whey is one of the best-known dairy proteins in sports nutrition.
health_impact_human: It can be a useful protein source, but it may still contain lactose. For people with lactose intolerance, that can mean bloating, gas, cramps, or diarrhea, especially in larger amounts.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs in small amounts, but too much may lead to gas or loose stool because dairy is not tolerated equally well by every dog.
health_impact_cat: Some cats may digest small amounts, but many adult cats handle dairy poorly and may get diarrhea, gas, or stomach discomfort.

what_is_it: A dried form of whey with concentrated dairy protein and flavor.
why_used: Used to add protein, creamy taste, and a more appealing texture.
fun_fact: Drying whey makes it easier to store, mix, and use in processed foods and treats.
health_impact_human: It can add useful protein, but it is more processed and may still cause bloating, gas, or diarrhea in people sensitive to lactose or dairy proteins. In very processed foods, it can also make the product feel healthier than it really is.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs in small amounts, though some dogs may get mild digestive upset if they do not handle dairy well.
health_impact_cat: Cats are often less tolerant of dairy ingredients, so whey powder may lead to loose stool or stomach discomfort in sensitive cats.

what_is_it: Milk that has been dried into a shelf-stable powder.
why_used: Used to add dairy flavor, protein, and calcium without using fresh milk.
fun_fact: It is common in processed foods because it is easy to store and easy to mix in.
health_impact_human: It still contains milk sugars and proteins, so for people with lactose intolerance it may cause bloating, cramps, gas, or diarrhea. It is nutritious, but not automatically easy to digest just because it is dried.
health_impact_dog: Some dogs can handle small amounts, but others may get loose stool, gas, or stomach upset because milk sugar can be hard to digest.
health_impact_cat: Many cats do not digest milk well, so milk powder can lead to diarrhea, stomach discomfort, or messy litter-box issues.

what_is_it: Milk powder with most of the fat removed.
why_used: Used to add dairy protein and minerals with less richness than full milk powder.
fun_fact: It keeps much of the protein and calcium while cutting back on fat.
health_impact_human: Lower fat does not mean easier to digest for everyone. It still contains lactose, so sensitive people may still get bloating, cramps, gas, or diarrhea.
health_impact_dog: May be tolerated by some dogs in small amounts, but lactose can still cause digestive upset, especially in dogs with sensitive stomachs.
health_impact_cat: Often not ideal for cats because they commonly struggle with lactose and may get loose stool or stomach discomfort.

what_is_it: A slow-digesting milk protein.
why_used: Used to add protein and help products feel more filling over time.
fun_fact: Casein digests more slowly than whey, which is why it is popular in some protein products.
health_impact_human: It can be a high-quality protein, but it still comes from milk. For people with milk allergy, it can trigger reactions, and for some sensitive people it may feel heavy or harder to digest.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs in small amounts. A sensitive dog could still show signs like itching, gas, or soft stool if it does not tolerate dairy proteins well.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats in small amounts, though very sensitive cats may still react to dairy-based proteins.

what_is_it: A processed milk protein made from casein.
why_used: Used to add protein and improve texture, making foods smoother and more stable.
fun_fact: Caseinate is often used in protein bars and creamy processed foods.
health_impact_human: It is a high-quality protein, but comes from milk and can trigger reactions in people with milk allergy. In sensitive individuals it may cause digestive discomfort or inflammation-like symptoms.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs in small amounts, but some dogs sensitive to dairy proteins may develop itching, gas, or loose stool.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats in small amounts, though some may still react to dairy proteins with mild digestive upset.

what_is_it: A natural sugar found in milk and dairy products.
why_used: Used as an energy source and naturally present in many dairy-based foods.
fun_fact: Many adults around the world have reduced ability to digest lactose.
health_impact_human: Many people cannot properly digest lactose, which can lead to bloating, gas, cramps, and diarrhea. The severity depends on individual tolerance.
health_impact_dog: Some dogs can tolerate small amounts, but others may experience gas, loose stool, or stomach upset.
health_impact_cat: Most adult cats are lactose intolerant, so lactose can easily cause diarrhea and digestive discomfort.

what_is_it: A dried form of cheese used as a flavor ingredient.
why_used: Used to make food taste richer, saltier, and more appealing.
fun_fact: It is often used to boost flavor in snacks without using fresh cheese.
health_impact_human: It contains salt, fat, and dairy compounds, so in excess it may contribute to high calorie intake and digestive discomfort, especially for people sensitive to dairy.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs in small amounts, but too much may lead to weight gain or digestive upset due to fat and salt.
health_impact_cat: Cats may enjoy the taste, but dairy and salt can cause digestive upset or diarrhea in sensitive individuals.

what_is_it: A dried version of yogurt with concentrated dairy components.
why_used: Used to add flavor, slight tanginess, and some nutrients in a convenient form.
fun_fact: Drying removes water but keeps much of the dairy content.
health_impact_human: It may contain lactose and dairy proteins, so it can still cause bloating, gas, or discomfort in people with lactose intolerance.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs in small amounts, though some may still react to dairy with mild digestive issues.
health_impact_cat: Cats may have trouble digesting dairy-based powders, which can lead to diarrhea or stomach discomfort.

what_is_it: A liquid dairy product left after butter is made.
why_used: Used to add flavor, slight acidity, and moisture to foods.
fun_fact: Traditional buttermilk is different from modern versions, which are often cultured.
health_impact_human: It contains lactose, so people with lactose intolerance may experience bloating, gas, cramps, or diarrhea.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs in small amounts, but larger amounts may cause digestive upset.
health_impact_cat: Cats often do not tolerate lactose well, so buttermilk may cause diarrhea or stomach discomfort.

what_is_it: The hard outer shell of an egg, rich in minerals.
why_used: Used as a natural source of calcium, especially in supplements and pet diets.
fun_fact: Eggshell is mostly made of calcium carbonate.
health_impact_human: It can be a good calcium source, but only when properly prepared and finely ground. Large or sharp pieces could irritate the digestive tract.
health_impact_dog: Safe for dogs when properly ground and used in balanced amounts, supporting bone health.
health_impact_cat: Safe for cats in small, properly prepared amounts and often used as a calcium supplement.

what_is_it: A thin layer inside the eggshell rich in proteins.
why_used: Used to support joints and connective tissue.
fun_fact: It naturally contains collagen and compounds linked to joint health.
health_impact_human: Generally considered beneficial, especially for joint support, with no major safety concerns in normal amounts.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs and often included in joint-support supplements.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats and may support joint health in small amounts.

what_is_it: A by-product left after brewing beer from grains.
why_used: Used to add fiber and some nutrients while reducing waste.
fun_fact: It is one of the most common recycled ingredients in animal nutrition.
health_impact_human: It can provide fiber, but it is a processed by-product and not very nutrient-dense. In excess, it may contribute to digestive imbalance or low-quality diet composition.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs and can support digestion, but very high amounts may lead to bulky stool or reduced nutrient absorption.
health_impact_cat: Generally safe for cats in small amounts, though it is not a key nutritional ingredient for them.

what_is_it: A by-product from alcohol production made from grains.
why_used: Used to provide protein and fiber in a cost-effective way.
fun_fact: It is commonly reused in animal feed instead of being wasted.
health_impact_human: It is safe but highly processed and not a premium ingredient. Overuse may mean lower overall food quality and less balanced nutrition.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs, but as a processed by-product it should not be a main protein source in high amounts.
health_impact_cat: Generally safe for cats in small amounts, though it offers limited nutritional value compared to fresh ingredients.

what_is_it: A fine starch extracted from corn.
why_used: Used to thicken, stabilize, and bind ingredients together.
fun_fact: It is one of the most widely used thickeners in processed foods.
health_impact_human: It provides quick energy but very little nutrition. In large amounts it can contribute to blood sugar spikes and lower overall nutritional quality of food.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs and easy to digest, but it mainly adds calories without much nutritional value.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats in small amounts, though it is not a necessary part of their diet and adds mostly carbohydrates.

what_is_it: A starch that has been chemically or physically changed to behave differently.
why_used: Used to improve texture, thickness, and shelf life in processed foods.
fun_fact: Modified starch is designed to stay stable under heat, freezing, or long storage.
health_impact_human: It is generally safe, but highly processed and adds little nutritional value. In large amounts it may contribute to blood sugar spikes and is a sign of ultra-processed food.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs, but mainly provides empty calories and can contribute to weight gain if overused.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats in small amounts, though it adds little nutritional benefit and is not essential in their diet.

what_is_it: A starch extracted from peas.
why_used: Used to thicken, bind, and add structure to food.
fun_fact: It is often used in grain-free products as an alternative to wheat or corn.
health_impact_human: It is easy to digest and generally safe, but it is mostly a source of carbohydrates and not very nutrient-rich.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs and commonly used in grain-free diets, though too much may lead to excess carbohydrate intake.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats in small amounts, but it is not a natural part of a high-protein feline diet.

what_is_it: A refined starch taken from potatoes.
why_used: Used to bind ingredients and improve texture in processed foods.
fun_fact: It is naturally gluten-free and widely used in gluten-free products.
health_impact_human: It is easy to digest but provides mostly quick carbohydrates, which can raise blood sugar quickly if eaten in large amounts.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs and easy on the stomach, but adds mostly calories without much nutrition.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats in small amounts, though it is not a necessary nutrient for them.

what_is_it: Oil extracted from plants such as sunflower, rapeseed, or soy.
why_used: Used to add fat, energy, and improve texture and taste.
fun_fact: Often a blend of different oils rather than a single clearly defined source.
health_impact_human: It provides essential fatty acids, but highly refined oils may lose some nutrients and in excess can contribute to weight gain and inflammation imbalance.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs and provides energy and fatty acids, but too much may lead to weight gain or digestive upset.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats and supports skin and coat health, but should be balanced with other fats.

what_is_it: Oil rendered from animal tissues.
why_used: Used to improve taste and provide energy and fats.
fun_fact: It is often added to make food more appealing due to its smell.
health_impact_human: It can provide useful fats, but quality depends on the source. In excess, it may contribute to high calorie intake and unhealthy fat balance.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs and improves palatability, but too much can lead to weight gain or greasy stools.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats and supports energy intake, but should be balanced to avoid excess fat consumption.

what_is_it: Fat obtained by cooking and processing animal tissues.
why_used: Used to add flavor, energy, and improve texture.
fun_fact: Rendering helps extend shelf life and stabilize the fat.
health_impact_human: It is safe but highly processed, and quality can vary. In excess it may contribute to weight gain and lower overall food quality.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs, but high amounts may lead to obesity or digestive issues like loose stool.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats, though too much fat can cause digestive upset or contribute to weight gain.

what_is_it: A traditional fat made from beef or mutton.
why_used: Used as an energy source and to enhance flavor.
fun_fact: It has been used in cooking for centuries.
health_impact_human: It provides energy and fat-soluble nutrients, but is high in saturated fat, which should be balanced in the diet.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs and provides energy, but too much can lead to weight gain.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats and can be a useful fat source in moderation.

what_is_it: Fat derived from pork.
why_used: Used to add flavor and energy to foods.
fun_fact: It has been a traditional cooking fat in many cultures.
health_impact_human: It is calorie-dense and high in fat, so overconsumption may contribute to weight gain and cardiovascular concerns if diet is unbalanced.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs in small amounts, but too much can lead to obesity or digestive upset.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats in moderation, but excess fat intake may cause digestive issues.

what_is_it: A fat that has been chemically altered to stay solid and last longer.
why_used: Used to improve shelf life and texture in processed foods.
fun_fact: This process can create trans fats, which are widely debated.
health_impact_human: It may contain trans fats, which are linked to increased risk of heart disease and inflammation when consumed regularly.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe in small regulated amounts, but not ideal for regular consumption and may affect long-term health.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe in small amounts, but not a desirable fat source and should be limited.

what_is_it: An emulsifier made from soy that helps ingredients blend smoothly.
why_used: Used to keep fats and liquids mixed and improve texture.
fun_fact: It is one of the most common emulsifiers in processed foods.
health_impact_human: Generally safe, but often highly processed and may cause issues for people with soy allergies. It mainly signals a more processed product.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs and used in small amounts to improve texture.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats and mainly used for texture, not nutrition.

what_is_it: An emulsifier made from sunflower seeds.
why_used: Used to help mix ingredients and create a smooth texture.
fun_fact: Often used as an alternative to soy lecithin.
health_impact_human: Generally safe and often better tolerated than soy lecithin, but still a processed additive rather than a whole-food ingredient.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs and used in small amounts for texture.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats and mainly used to stabilize food.

what_is_it: A substance that helps oil and water mix together.
why_used: Used to keep food smooth, stable, and evenly blended.
fun_fact: Without emulsifiers, many foods would separate and look unappealing.
health_impact_human: Generally safe, but indicates processed food. Some people prefer to limit them due to concerns about gut health with heavy long-term intake.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs and used in very small amounts.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats and mainly helps maintain texture.

what_is_it: An additive that keeps food texture consistent over time.
why_used: Used to prevent separation and maintain a uniform structure.
fun_fact: Helps products stay the same from production to consumption.
health_impact_human: Generally safe, but another sign of processing. High intake of many additives together may affect gut comfort in sensitive individuals.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs and used in small amounts to keep food consistent.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats and helps maintain texture stability.

what_is_it: An ingredient that makes liquids thicker.
why_used: Used to improve texture and mouthfeel in foods.
fun_fact: Common in sauces, soups, and many processed meals.
health_impact_human: Usually safe, but highly refined thickeners add little nutrition and may cause digestive discomfort in sensitive people if consumed in large amounts.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs and helps improve food texture.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats and commonly used in wet food.

what_is_it: An ingredient that forms a gel-like texture.
why_used: Used to give structure to foods like jelly or wet pet food.
fun_fact: It helps create the familiar shape and consistency of many products.
health_impact_human: Generally safe, but often part of highly processed foods. Some people may experience mild digestive sensitivity depending on the type used.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs and commonly used in wet food.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats and helps create texture in moist foods.

what_is_it: An ingredient that holds food together.
why_used: Used to maintain shape and structure, especially in processed products.
fun_fact: Essential in products like kibble to keep pieces intact.
health_impact_human: Generally safe, but indicates processing and often adds little nutritional value.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs and important for food structure.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats and mainly serves a structural purpose.

what_is_it: An ingredient that helps retain moisture in food.
why_used: Used to keep products soft and prevent them from drying out.
fun_fact: Common in soft treats and semi-moist foods.
health_impact_human: Generally safe, but often associated with highly processed foods. Some types may cause mild digestive discomfort in sensitive individuals.
health_impact_dog: Usually safe for dogs and helps keep treats soft, though not nutritionally important.
health_impact_cat: Usually safe for cats and mainly affects texture rather than nutrition.
